title: South Indian Masala Vada

subs.name: an equal quantity of masoor dal (red lentil) soaked for 1 hour makes a slightly softer, lighter vada
a pinch of kalonji (nigella seeds) adds a different aromatic note; dried curry leaf powder also works at half the quantity
half a teaspoon of cumin seeds as a straightforward swap; the vada's character shifts slightly but remains delicious

steps.text: Drain the soaked chana dal and reserve a small handful of whole soaked dal aside — this will be folded in at the end to create a textured vada. Place the remaining dal in a blender or wet grinder and grind coarsely using as little water as possible (add just a teaspoon at a time if the blades stall). The batter should be rough and thick, not smooth — you want visible dal fragments. Do not over-grind.

Transfer the ground dal to a large bowl. Add the reserved whole dal, chopped onion, green chilli, grated ginger, curry leaves, coriander leaves, fennel seeds, dried red chilli (crumbled), asafoetida, and salt. Mix thoroughly with your hands, pressing everything together. The mixture should hold its shape when squeezed into a ball — if it seems too wet, add a spoonful of rice flour to tighten it. Taste the raw mixture and adjust seasoning.

Shape the vadas: wet your hands and take a portion of the mixture (roughly 2 tablespoons). Press it firmly between your palms to form a flat round patty about 6 cm in diameter and 1.5 cm thick. Make a small hole in the centre with your thumb if you prefer the traditional ring shape, though flat discs work equally well. Repeat to form all the vadas.

Pour oil into a deep kadai to a depth of 8 cm and heat to 170°C over medium flame. Test by dropping a small piece of batter — it should rise slowly within 3 seconds. Slide in 4–5 vadas at a time. Fry over medium heat for 4–5 minutes per side, turning once, until the vadas are a deep amber colour all over and feel firm when pressed. Do not rush the frying over high heat — a slow fry at moderate temperature is what gives masala vada its characteristic crunchy crust and cooked-through interior.

Drain on kitchen paper and serve hot immediately. Masala vada pairs perfectly with fresh coconut chutney and a cup of strong filter coffee. They lose their crunch within 20–30 minutes, so fry only as many as will be eaten fresh.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep water away from hot oil, lower food gently, avoid overfilling the vessel and never leave frying unattended.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: chana-dal → an equal quantity of masoor dal (red lentil) soaked for 1 hour makes a slightly softer, lighter vada; fennel → half a teaspoon of cumin seeds as a straightforward swap; the vada's character shifts slightly but remains delicious; curry-leaves → a pinch of kalonji (nigella seeds) adds a different aromatic note; dried curry leaf powder also works at half the quantity
